speaker: interviewer
text: OK. February 4, 1996. The interview is with Frances Flumenbaum. Last name at birth-- maiden name, Blady. Interviewed by Mary Rothschild in Beverly Hills, California, USA, speaking English. Tape number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is February 4, 1996. I'm Mary Rothschild, interviewing Frances Flumenbaum for The Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. [INTERPOSING VOICES] [INAUDIBLE] This is February 4th, 1996. Tape one with Frances Flumenbaum. Please, give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Frances Flumenbaum. F-L-U-M-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frances. F-R-A-N-C-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 10th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blady. B-L-A-D-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. The name of the city is Slawkow. S-L-A-W-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember about my childhood. I was born to a very orthodox father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine father's name was Wolf Ber and my mother's name was Bela. Bela [NON-ENGLISH]. You want me to tell you now what I remember? OK. I remember being raised very orthodox. And as a child, I not knowing even being uncomfortable and why. I did go through my life till I understood because I respected and loved my parents highly. And I thought, whatever they tell you, this is the way how it should be. I loved them dearly. I came from a home where we were comfortable making a living. My father was a scholar, my mother also educated in several languages. I do remember my mother giving lessons in Hebrew and in Russia in order to make a little bit more comfortable our life. We had a grocery store. And my mother helped in the store because my father was a scholar. I also remember every Saturday, people are coming in to our house, and listened, and learn from my father a lot. At that time, I was young enough, but I liked to listen also. And I was sitting in a corner where they didn't even see me, because, as a girl, I wasn't allowed to be in the same room. But I liked it a lot to listen whatever they had to say, at that time, whatever I understood. When I started regular school, I also two years before, I start Hebrew and Jewish education. I remember a lot. When I did play outside with my friends, it was very pleasant. I liked to play. And my father used to say, you come on in because your rabbi is in here waiting for you. And when I saw that rabbi, I didn't like him to come and take me away. But my father said-- and this, I always remember the rest of my life-- anything what you possess, you might lose in your life, but not what will sticks to your brains. Until today, I know so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I'm with a group of people where they asking, who knows how to read Hebrew and who knows how to read Jewish? I can raise my hand, because I know. And this is thanks because my parents put a lot of effort in education. Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living in Poland? I did. Going to school, a lots of time-- especially when came before the holidays, Christmas-- we felt it, that we are Jewish, because we were afraid to go out in the street. And also remember, very well, being told, when I got in a sort of dispute or something with a girlfriend at school, that she used to say, why-- if you don't like it here, why don't you go back to your own country? But you can't, because you don't have it. And that's why today, with me-- I can pick up my head high enough. And proud that I am able-- and thankful to God-- to contribute today to work what I'm doing to keep up our country. And our children, grandchildren, and forever generations to come should have a country. And Israel should be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances, did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister's name was Sarah. My brother's name was David. And the youngest one, who was only six when the war broke out, he-- his name was Hersch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your relationship with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Very, very close. I loved them dearly. I was the oldest one. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] And I remember taking care on them, helping out when they went to school. And at that time, was not so easy, especially during the war. And I was watching over them because I felt, I'm the oldest one. In case something happened, I am here for them. And thanks God, I survived with one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to share your room with any one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We shared-- we shared the room. If there were the little brothers or the sisters, when we had to make the homework, if the kitchen was free, or the little room what we had around, or even the bedroom, we did share. We knew to share not only the place, to share the food and to share the clothes. And I remember this very well about sharing, because every Friday, my father used to say to me, before gets Shabbos, you take a package-- was prepared a package-- to go to a-- a-- a home where the homeless were. And I used to come. It wasn't like here-- homeless on the street. A homeless had a home where to sleep overnight. And being supported over Shabbat with food, that he shouldn't have to go beg around. Or my father used to bring somebody from the shul. And we always had people. And we always had to share what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Shabbat dinners in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat dinners-- they are Friday afternoon. The Thursday, started already with the spirit of Shabbat. The baking and the cooking-- we were five in the family. Five children and the parents was seven. And always somebody else coming from the outside. So my mother was pretty busy. My father helped us, the children, to get out from the house to school, to do little things, what we could to help out. And Friday, the cooking. And the Shabbat was a holy evening, a evening relaxation. I felt, being a child, I felt, at that time, that somebody is watching over us. Nothing can happen. We sitting and celebrating a meal what God gave us. And my father, he always was proper dressed as a Hasid. He was a big member of the Chasidim, so he had his own shtreimel, we called it at that time-- that hat particular. The long clothes what he was wearing. I admired this and I cherished this. And I loved that to hear the way he was-- my mother [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My father used to give us a kiss and say, shabbat shalom. We didn't celebrate in a rich way, but close, with a lot of love. And this stays in me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about other holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: About other holidays-- we were surrounded, in a small town, with the closest family. My father's sister and my father's parents were living in the same town. At that time, in Poland, people didn't send parents to old age homes. They kept them in the house and cherished them. And every Shabbat-- even though it was Shabbat, a walking distance, we could walk. I used to run to say to them, shabbat shalom. They kissed me and they asked me to come back for cookies, like our grandmother used to do it and still doing it. And I remember the holidays we really spent private, because my father wouldn't mix our Passover, for instance. He had this matzah [NON-ENGLISH] the matzah [NON-ENGLISH] made by himself, and he wouldn't mix the eight days, not even with his own parents. Even though, you're going to see later on, the picture of my grandfather, who still exists in Poland, lying next to the rabbi because he was honored. And with honesty, he was buried in [NON-ENGLISH]. If you go and see it, says he died like they-- with honesty, like they say here, for George Washington, he never said a lie. And this was my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersch. My brother carried his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother lost [INAUDIBLE] her parents when she was very young. I did not get to know my mother's parents. I just heard about them. And I-- I remember my mother's two brothers also. Living in Krakow. Very very, very religious home. At that time, this is the way how they used to get married-- sect with the sect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish in the house. Only. I never heard my father saying a word Polish-- to each other, of course. Only to customers. They used to come in-- in the store when it was necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked at school, because-- particular liked at school when subjects like history. I loved that. But I was sort of uncomfortable because when I started to feel the way that you are Jewish, you are different. Even though the teachers were very nice to every student, it's just like sometimes you had the feeling, being among the boys, mostly boys-- the girls behaved a little bit better in school, more decent. I liked at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any books in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Books of what? Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- first of all, I always repeated the Jewish history. I do that. Sometimes I go through the Chumash. The Bereishit is the first one which I got to know a little bit more than the others. And I liked to read, in general. Right now, I'm reading a little bit lighter, but I used to read more history books, because this was in my mind always. And I was interested, especially in the heritage, and the life of our forefathers, and forever. How-- how we are raised-- this is the way my parents all wanted-- don't forget who your forefathers were and what's your heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember about my mother being a good looking woman, very stunny, and helping my father a lot in the store. Sharing, I should say, with my father, because my father was the one, in the morning, to check us going out to school, if we dressed proper, if everything is done, if we have our lunches with, what to do in school, how to obey. And my mother already was busy in the kitchen, and up in the store, and help, out because my father was a lot spending a lot of time in his shul, teaching and always learn more and more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the first incidents of anti-semitism that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my life, the-- the first-- well, like I said, as a child in the school I remember little things, especially when the-- those holidays came with-- you know what I mean. And then when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1939. And I remember we start to prepare ourselves with food, of course. And then I remember they said we should leave town and run to another city. They thought the other city's going to be safe which was next to ours. And we did so. We closed up everything, and we went to the other city. I remember coming back. When they saw this doesn't help, and they sent back all the Jewish people from that city to their own-- back home. I remember there was a bridge. And the Poles and the German controlled the people who should pass the bridge, and who should be thrown into the water to make it less. And this I remember, standing next to my father. My father was very known, very well known. Not only by the Jewish people, it was a small town anyway, but also by the Poles. And they said, well, he is a holy man. We will let them go. And that's the way how my father came back home. And then we were back home, of course, struggling, like anybody else, with the ghetto. Closed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in your own home or did you have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We stayed. We stayed in our own home. I mean, I stayed in our home till, 19-- I think '41, something like that. I don't remember exactly the dates. Till they took me to jail. I can tell you the story about the jail, because this is in my mind. We were still trying. The store was closed. We couldn't do anything. Whatever we had, we had. And with the Poles living around us, they helped ourselves. They used to come to the house, exchange things, and bringing. The ones who knew us before, they pitied us with having five children in the house. We always had a little bit support from them, from certain one. And then my father said I should get a passport from the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], they called them at that time. I should get a passport and travel to Sosnowiec, which was half hour distance by train. And from over there, I should-- will get connections to bring home linen-- linen was very thin and hard to get at home-- to put on me. I always was very skinny. And I could take-- as much I could, I took. And this was in Sosnowiec, in a Jewish home. And when I came home, because I had that linen, we-- we were able, like, to help ourselves. The Poles from around came. They wanted the linen. They brought us food. And this was going on, and on, and on for months. Till one time, I got back and the-- the German people-- somebody probably said something. The German people were waiting for us and they took us all to the [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] is the building where the German were commanding, being there. And of course, they asked right away, how-- what do you have? And I did say, I have this and this on me. They took it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not punish me, for-- because for respect of my parents, especially my father. So they didn't punish me. They just asked me the question, who gave it to me Sosnowiec and where's the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home to the house, my father said, you will have to go through a court. And the main thing what they will like to know from you, that will be from whom you got this, because whatever has to happen to you, it's going to happen anyway. But that Jewish family what I know so well, first of all, the man will be hanged on the street-- this was the time when they were hanging people and leave them hanging. They didn't take them off. And this going be the first thing what they're going to do. So soon, you're going to tell from where. And I kept this in my mind. A month later, they took me to Sosnowiec to a court.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the judge asked me the question, he said, I will repeat 10 times, where did you got? And if you're going tell me where, I'll let you go home. And you're not going be punished, you're going to be surviving with your parents, sisters, and brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father, whatever he said, there was a strong belief in me, that if they would kill me, I wouldn't have said it. So one German who was sitting next to me, he lit a cigarette. And he said, and I'm sure now you're going to tell me. And he put that burning cigarette on my hands. I still have the marks here. At the third time, I was-- was hurting, but I started to cry a little bit. But in me, I was so strong that I won't tell, because I saw the picture what's going happen. And I said no, I'll survive. And I didn't tell them. I didn't. And a month later, they took me to jail for a half a year. Going to jail, I remember also my mother packing a suitcase for me to take along, in case something happened, like I couldn't come home anymore. And they were, for a month, praying in the house that nothing should happen to me. This I remember, especially prayer my father put. Besides, praying was going on all the time, and not legal. And I remember the day. It was snowing terribly in the winter. They came in 7 o'clock in the morning for my father to shovel the snow. They cut his beard as much you're going to see on the picture. They left him a little bit. And my-- and then they came for me, a little bit later, to transfer me to jail. This I also won't forget. I was passing by, going to the station, and my father was shoveling the snow. My father knew where they going take me because this was the time when he knew they go and transfer me to jail, with two Germans and one Jewish person who was with me. And I passed by. And I said to that Jewish person, let me give a hug, a kiss my father. This, he said, you cannot do. You just can close by him. And while I was close by, my father picked up his hat and he went like that. I knew exactly what he meant. No matter what's going happen to you, never say who gave you this. They took me to jail. The name of the place was Wadowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1941, something like that. And then I came into jail, of course. I came-- this was on a Friday. And they kept me in a Jewish family over Saturday, also because they knew who I am. And Sunday morning, I rang the bell. And their doorman opened and he let me into jail. They gave me-- the job what I had was a good job, I have to say, because I was working in a garden from fruit-- picking fruit. So-- and since I knew already a little bit of German, because I took in school, and Polish, so I was translating to the Polish women to the Germans what-- so it wasn't too bad. While I was there, maybe three months later, somebody came also from our own town. I don't remember what they did, but they were punished with jail. And they told me they-- it was the last day before I left that they took all the people. They separate the parents, children. And younger people to work, to ghettos. And the older ones-- so your parents, and your sisters, and you younger two brothers, they are gone. You will never see them again. And then I finished the jail. And I had a armband with the star. And when I walked out that jail, the German who stood at the door, the doorman, said to me like that, you don't have to wear that now. Nobody's going watch it. So this means he meant that if I'm not going wear it, I can survive as a Christian. And I did try. I took it off, and I was walking around in Sosnowiec and seeing what's happened to our people. I knew somewhere is my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, should I survive as a Christian? Why should I do it? Running away from my own family, from my upbringing, from my parents. No, I'm going to look for my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few days, I went into the ghetto again. I ringing a bell, going into a ghetto. There were my sister. We were together. And then we were for a while in ghetto. Each time when it was an appel, they [SIGHS] were like this. I guess everybody knows already how they separated people. Younger, tearing away the children, throwing them on the trucks, screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances, we have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
